A Mr. Justice Marshall, if the State possesses identity and the record is slim as to what leads the police could get from Mrs. Key. 1 believe the original Mississippi Supreme Court, hospital bed with bruises about the face and 1 think on the neck, and it is just my conjecture that in her age and having gone through what she had she was not in a position to give very much information. But suffice to say, our State Supreme Court went along with my argument that this boy, and incidentally he was only youth that he didnst gat the death penalty. I was waiting to get to where you were going to distinguish» Q The distinction is set out in Schmerber versus California and other cases cited therein. Q Your adversary is not relying one bit on Miranda. That is the Fifth Amendment. 0 That is right. A But I am getting back to the Fourth Amendment fact-finding process. Q Do you contend that his appearance at the. police A Mr. Justice, I wouldn't say it was voluntary. I don't know. Q So they would have brought him down there -™- A Fourteen year-old boy? Q Yes» A I can say but 1 wouldn't say it was voluntary. Q Do you. think that the question whether that was an "arrest" is crucial in the case? A I think it does. 1 think that is the question.
